sources.verified: The functions that could possibly have given function as a derivative are called anti derivatives (or primitive) of the function.

sources.verified: An antiderivative, F, of a function, f, can be defined as a function that can be differentiated to obtain the original function, f.

concepts.contentNote: Solid. The +C non-uniqueness in PART 2 mirrors NCERT exactly ('anti derivatives ... are not unique'). Optional: the worked example uses 2x -> x^2, but the NCERT motivating triple is d/dx(sin x)=cos x, d/dx(x^3/3)=x^2, d/dx(e^x)=e^x; aligning the example with one of those (e.g. x^2 from x^3/3) would tie the bedrock node directly to the source quotes students will see again in section 7.2.

concepts.contentNote: Accurate and well-scoped. PART 2's claim that 'chain rule reversed becomes substitution; product rule reversed becomes integration by parts' is correct and is borne out later in the chapter (section 7.3.1 derives substitution as the change-of-variable formula; section 7.6 derives by-parts from the product rule). No change needed.

concepts.contentNote: Correct. The 'NOT a number (that is the definite integral)' contrast in PART 2 is pedagogically valuable and consistent with section 7.7. No change needed.

concepts.contentNote: Excellent worked example (t = x^2+1 for ∫ 2x(x^2+1) dx) matches NCERT Example 5(ii) in spirit. The two stated misconceptions ('not converting dx into dt' and 'not substituting back') are precisely the errors NCERT flags. No change needed.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: If the degree of P(x) is less than the degree of Q(x), then the rational function is called proper, otherwise, it is called improper.

sources.title: Integration using Partial Fractions — BYJU'S
sources.verified: If not, perform polynomial long division first

refreshers.trigger: Student must compare the degree of the numerator with the degree of the denominator to classify a rational function as proper or improper, but may not recall that 'degree' means the highest power of x.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: In the polynomial 3x^2 - 5x + 7, what is its 'degree'?
ladder: A polynomial is a sum of terms, each a constant times a power of x: e.g. 7 (= 7x^0), -5x (= -5x^1), 3x^2.
The 'degree' of a polynomial is the highest power of x that appears with a non-zero coefficient.
So in 3x^2 - 5x + 7, the powers present are 2, 1, and 0. The highest is 2.
Watch the leading coefficient is irrelevant to the degree: 100x + 1 has degree 1, while x^2 has degree 2, even though 100 > 1.
refreshers.answer: The degree of 3x^2 - 5x + 7 is 2, because the highest power of x present is x^2. Degree counts the highest exponent of x, not the size of any coefficient. (Examples: x^2/(x-1) has numerator degree 2 and denominator degree 1, so it is improper; (x+1)/(x^2-4) has numerator degree 1 < denominator degree 2, so it is proper.)
refreshers.returnCue: Now that 'degree' = highest power of x, compare deg(numerator) with deg(denominator): proper if numerator's is smaller, improper otherwise.

concepts.contentNote: Correct and matches NCERT 7.5 verbatim. The flagged misconception 'comparing leading coefficients instead of degrees' is well-chosen. The node's only stated prereq is integration-inverse-of-differentiation; the genuinely load-bearing prior skill is 'degree of a polynomial' (pre-curriculum bedrock) — see attached refresher.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: It is always possible to write the integrand as a sum of simpler rational functions by a method called partial fraction decomposition.

sources.title: Integration by Partial Fractions - Method, Examples & Practice Problems — GeeksforGeeks
sources.verified: If degree f(x) < degree g(x), then f(x)/g(x) is called a proper rational function.

sources.title: Integration using Partial Fractions — BYJU'S
sources.verified: If not, perform polynomial long division first

refreshers.trigger: Partial fraction decomposition requires first factoring the denominator into linear and quadratic factors; a student who cannot factor a quadratic like x^2 - 5x + 6 cannot set up the decomposition.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: How would you write x^2 - 5x + 6 as a product of two factors (x - ?)(x - ?)?
ladder: To factor x^2 + bx + c into (x - p)(x - q), look for two numbers p and q whose product is c and whose sum is -b (i.e. p + q = -b, pq = c).
For x^2 - 5x + 6: we need two numbers that multiply to +6 and add to +5 (since -b = 5).
Try pairs that multiply to 6: 1 and 6 (sum 7), 2 and 3 (sum 5). The pair 2 and 3 works.
So x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x - 2)(x - 3). Check by expanding: (x - 2)(x - 3) = x^2 - 5x + 6.
refreshers.answer: x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x - 2)(x - 3). Factoring a monic quadratic x^2 + bx + c means finding two numbers with product c and sum b; here product 6 and sum -5 gives -2 and -3, so the factors are (x - 2) and (x - 3). The factored denominator is what each partial fraction is written over: 1/((x-2)(x-3)) = A/(x-2) + B/(x-3).
refreshers.returnCue: With the denominator factored into (x - 2)(x - 3), write one partial fraction per factor (A/(x-2) + B/(x-3)) and solve for the constants.

concepts.contentNote: Accurate. The standard forms quoted in PART 1 (A/(x-a); A/(x-a)+B/(x-a)^2; (Ax+B)/quadratic) exactly match NCERT Table 7.2. Worked example 1/((x-1)(x-2)) = -1/(x-1)+1/(x-2) is correct. Genuine pre-curriculum bedrock for this node is 'factoring polynomials/quadratics' — refresher attached; this is owned upstream conceptually (Class 10 algebra) and is not taught in cbse12 ch01-ch06.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: The integral of the product of two functions = (first function) × (integral of the second function) – Integral of [(differential coefficient of the first function) × (integral of the second function)]

sources.title: Integration by Parts Rule: Easy Steps & Examples — Vedantu
sources.verified: The term which is closer to I is differentiated first and the term which is closer to E is integrated first.

sources.title: Integration by Parts - Formula, Derivation, Applications, Examples — Cuemath
sources.verified: ∫ u dv = uv - ∫ v du

concepts.contentNote: Correct. PART 2's ILATE ordering (Inverse-trig, Log, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential) is the standard mnemonic; note NCERT itself does not name 'ILATE' but states the equivalent rule (Remark iii: take power-of-x/polynomial as first function, but if an inverse-trig or log function is present take that as first) — the node's ILATE framing is the widely-taught generalisation and is fine. Worked example ∫ x cos x dx = x sin x + cos x + C matches NCERT Example 17 exactly.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: The definite integral is introduced either as the limit of a sum or if it has an anti derivative F in the interval [a, b], then its value is the difference between the values of F at the end points, i.e., F(b) - F(a).

sources.title: Definite Integral - Calculate, Formula, Properties — Cuemath
sources.verified: A definite integral is the area under a curve between two fixed limits. The definite integral is represented as ∫ᵇₐf(x)dx, where a is the lower limit and b is the upper limit

sources.title: Integrals for Class 12 — Concepts — BYJU'S
sources.verified: A definite integral is denoted by ∫ab f(x) dx, where a is called the lower limit of the integral and b is called the upper limit of the integral.

concepts.contentNote: Accurate. PART 2's two contrasts (no +C; swapping limits negates the value) are both correct and exam-relevant; the sign-flip property is confirmed by Cuemath ('∫ᵇₐ f dx = -∫ₐᵇ f dx'). The 'single NUMBER vs family' contrast against the indefinite integral is the key takeaway. No change needed.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: Let f be a continuous function on the closed interval [a, b] and let A(x) be the area function. Then A'(x) = f(x), for all x ∈ [a, b].

sources.title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — BYJU'S
sources.verified: Then A′(x) = f(x), for all x ∈ [a, b].

refreshers.trigger: The area function A(x) = integral of f from a to x is defined as 'the accumulated area under the curve'; a student who does not picture a definite integral as area under a curve cannot grasp why differentiating accumulated area returns the height f(x).
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: When we write the definite integral of f from a to b, what geometric quantity does it measure?
ladder: Draw the graph of y = f(x). Mark two vertical lines at x = a and x = b.
The definite integral of f from a to b is the area of the region trapped between the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and those two vertical lines (taking area above the axis as positive).
Now let the right edge move: instead of fixing it at b, let it sit at a variable point x. The trapped area now grows or shrinks as x moves — so the area is a function of x, written A(x).

Intuitively, when x increases by a tiny step, the extra sliver of area added is roughly (height of curve) × (width of step) = f(x) × (small step). Dividing the extra area by the step size gives back the height f(x) — that is exactly A'(x) = f(x).
refreshers.answer: The definite integral of f from a to b measures the (signed) area of the region bounded by the curve y = f(x), the x-axis, and the vertical lines x = a and x = b. Letting the upper limit be a variable x makes that area a function A(x), the 'area-so-far' function. Because each tiny increase in x adds a thin strip of area of height f(x), the rate at which the accumulated area grows equals the curve's height: A'(x) = f(x). This is the First Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
refreshers.returnCue: Holding the picture 'integral = area under the curve', read A(x) as area-so-far and A'(x) = f(x) as: the speed at which area accumulates equals the curve's current height.

concepts.contentNote: Correct. The flagged misconception 'thinking A'(x) gives f(a) or a constant rather than f(x)' is the precise trap and is well-chosen. PART 1's definition of A(x) as the definite integral from fixed a to variable x matches NCERT 7.8.1 exactly. The continuity hypothesis is correctly retained. 'Area under a curve' is pre-curriculum geometric intuition load-bearing here — refresher attached.

sources.title: Integrals (NCERT Class XII Mathematics Part I, Chapter 7) — ncert.nic.in
sources.verified: Let f be continuous function defined on the closed interval [a, b] and F be an anti derivative of f. Then ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = [F(x)]ₐᵇ = F(b) - F(a).

sources.title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — BYJU'S
sources.verified: F(b)- F(a) = a∫b f(x) dx

sources.title: Integrals for Class 12 — Concepts — BYJU'S
sources.verified: ∫ab f(x) dx= [F(x)]ab = F(b) – F(a)

concepts.contentNote: Goal node is accurate and complete. Worked example (∫ x^2 from 1 to 3 = 26/3) is arithmetically correct and matches the structure of NCERT Example 25(i). PART 1's instruction to omit +C 'since it cancels' is justified verbatim by NCERT's step list ('There is no need to keep integration constant C ... the arbitrary constant disappears'). The flagged sign error F(a)-F(b) is the canonical exam mistake. No change needed.

loadBearingTerms: derivative / f'(x) (the operation integration reverses) — owned upstream: cbse12:mathematics:mathematics-ch05:derivative-definition
product rule (reversed -> integration by parts) — owned upstream: ch05 product/derivative rules
derivatives of e^x and log x (needed to read standard integrals backwards and for by-parts of log x) — owned upstream: cbse12:mathematics:mathematics-ch05:derivative-of-exp-and-log
inverse-trig derivatives (1/(1+x^2) -> arctan; 1/sqrt(1-x^2) -> arcsin) — owned upstream: cbse12:mathematics:mathematics-ch05:inverse-trig-derivatives
standard derivatives of sin, cos, tan, sec, etc. (read backwards as standard integrals) — owned upstream: ch05 differentiation
degree of a polynomial (proper vs improper classification) — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 9-10 algebra)
polynomial long division (reducing an improper rational function) — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 9-10 algebra)
factoring polynomials / quadratics into linear & quadratic factors (partial-fraction setup) — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 10 algebra)
rational function P(x)/Q(x), Q(x) != 0 — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 10-11 algebra)
natural logarithm log|x| and its absolute-value domain (appears in nearly every integral answer) — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 11 logarithms) reinforced by ch05
area under a curve / area bounded by a graph (definite integral & area function) — pre-curriculum geometric intuition

trigonometric identities (cos 2x, product-to-sum, sin^3x) used to recast integrands in 7.3.2 — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 11 trigonometry); note: not surfaced as a node in this chapter's content.json
completing the square (for ax^2+bx+c integrals in 7.4) — pre-curriculum bedrock (Class 10-11 algebra); not surfaced as a node
continuity of a function on [a,b] (hypothesis of both FTCs) — owned upstream: cbse12 ch05 continuity
bridgesProposed: NONE. No load-bearing idea in this chapter is taught nowhere upstream. The most foundational dependency — that integration reverses differentiation — is explicitly the inverse of cbse12:mathematics:mathematics-ch05 differentiation and is itself one of this chapter's bedrock nodes (integration-inverse-of-differentiation), so it needs no author-bridge. All other course concepts are owned by ch05 (differentiation, chain/product rules, exp/log & inverse-trig derivatives, continuity); the remainder are pre-curriculum bedrock handled via refreshers, not bridges.

defers.term: derivative / f'(x) and the meaning of differentiation
defers.softening: The antiderivative and inverse-of-differentiation nodes lean on 'what function differentiates to f?'. Defer the formal definition to ch05; soften with a one-line reminder ('recall f'(x) is the derivative from Chapter 5') rather than re-teaching.
Integration-by-substitution is 'the chain rule run backwards'. Defer the chain rule itself to ch05; soften by naming it as the rule being reversed when introducing substitution.
Integration-by-parts is derived by integrating the product rule (NCERT 7.6). Defer the product rule to ch05; soften by quoting d/dx(uv)=u v'+v u' as a recalled fact at the start of the by-parts node.

defers.term: derivatives of e^x, a^x, log|x| and inverse-trig functions
defers.softening: The table of standard integrals is just these ch05 derivatives read backwards. Defer ownership to ch05; soften by presenting each standard integral beside its known derivative.
Both FTC nodes carry a continuity hypothesis. Defer the definition to ch05; soften with a brief 'f must be continuous (no breaks) on [a,b]' gloss where the theorems are stated.

inlineGlosses: proper / improper rational function: gloss inline at proper-improper-rational — 'proper = numerator's highest power is smaller than the denominator's; improper otherwise' (the node already does this well; keep one-line).

integrand / variable of integration / constant of integration: gloss inline at indefinite-integral using NCERT Table 7.1 wording ('integrand' = f(x) inside the integral sign; 'C' = any real constant).
lower limit / upper limit a, b: gloss inline at definite-integral ('a = lower limit, b = upper limit of the integral').
irreducible quadratic factor: gloss inline at partial-fractions — 'a quadratic that cannot be factored into real linear factors (e.g. x^2+1)', so its partial fraction takes the (Ax+B)/quadratic form.
log|x| (absolute value in the log): gloss inline wherever 1/x integrates to log|x| — 'the |x| keeps the log defined for negative x as well', per NCERT Example 1(iii).
